Listen 2026-06-29

New requirements and changes regarding the process of estate inventory come into effect on July 1st. Here, we have gathered information for those who need to create estate inventories in their professional or personal capacity.

All individuals summoned to the proceedings must be identified by name, national identification number, or coordination number. If neither of these is available, please provide the date of birth.

The estate inventory must be submitted to the Tax Agency within four months of the death and serves as the deceased's estate's official document.

A registered estate inventory is required for:

To reduce the risk of supplementary information being needed:

The inventory form and brochure for estate declarations include a checklist.

On July 1, 2026, new legislation will come into effect that allows the Swedish Tax Agency (Skatteverket) to develop a digital solution for submitting estate declarations and death notifications. The Swedish Tax Agency is working on developing this digital solution.

This means that digital submission will only be possible once the Swedish Tax Agency has developed and launched a digital solution for users to access. Until then, estate declarations and death notifications must continue to be submitted in writing, in physical form.

Estate Declaration
tldr: Starting July 1st, there will be new regulations regarding how to create an inventory list (bouppteckning), which is the document that details everything a deceased person owned.  It will also become easier to submit the necessary documents digitally in the future.
lattlast:
An inventory of assets and liabilities is an important document that shows what a deceased person owned and what debts they had. From July 1st, there will be new rules for how these documents should be written. It's crucial that all individuals involved in the estate are listed by name and personal identification number to ensure the information is accurate.

This document must be submitted to the Swedish Tax Agency (Skatteverket), which is the government agency responsible for this process, within four months of the death. A registered inventory is necessary to demonstrate who has the right to make decisions regarding the deceased person's assets.

Starting July 1st, 2026, the Swedish Tax Agency may begin using digital solutions to receive these documents online. Until then, all documents must still be submitted in paper form.
